coredata.dc:title: Enhanced state of health prediction for lithium-ion batteries using a hybrid convolutional–Kolmogorov–Arnold network
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of ESG.
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Yangke
Feng, Yongzheng
coredata.dc:description: Reliable estimation of the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is essential to ensure the continuous and safe operation of energy-dependent systems. Traditional methods for LIB SOH estimation using neural networks often lacked the flexibility required to handle high-dimensional data, resulting in restricted accuracy. To address this issue, this study proposes a hybrid convolutional neural network (CNN)–Kolmogorov–Arnold network (KAN) model to provide a more accurate estimation of LIB SOH. With its learnable activation functions, KANs overcome the inflexibility challenges of traditional neural networks and have the potential to replace conventional multilayer perceptrons (MLP). In this study, five indirect health features (IHFs) were extracted from the charge–discharge voltage and capacity enhancement curves. To address the issue of weak informative content in IHFs, we try to combine the CNN’s powerful high-dimensional feature extraction capabilities to optimize the IHFs, thereby improving the prediction performance of the KAN. Convolutional layers are employed to extract local features from the IHFs, whereas pooling layers further reduce their dimensionality while preserving crucial information. This optimization enhances the feature set and significantly improves prediction accuracy. Finally, the optimized features are fed into the KAN model for SOH estimation. The SHAP technique was applied to calculate the contribution of each IHF to address the black-box nature and lack of interpretability inherent in data-driven models. This study uses publicly available datasets provided by CALCE, Oxford, as well as the Toyota Research Institute and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) for algorithm validation. The experimental results demonstrate that the CNN–KAN model performs excellently, with an root mean squared error (RMSE) less than 0.007, mean absolute error (MAE) less than 0.005, and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) below 0.006. Compared with the errors in other studies, the results highlight the strong predictive accuracy and reliability of the CNN-KAN model.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Kolmogorov–Arnold network
State-of-health
SHAP
Lithium-ion batteries

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Charge voltage curve of CS2_35 battery.
Discharge voltage curve of CS2_35 battery.
Prediction result of Set4 and Set5.
Prediction result of Set1, Set2 and Set3.
Interaction relationship between IHF2 and IHF4 of Set 1.
Interaction relationship between IHF1 and IHF3 of Set 1.
Pearson coefficient of CALCE between IHF and SOH.

row.entry: units = 64, grid size = 5, spline order: int = 3, grid range (−1.0, 1.0), basis activation = ‘relu’, spline_initialize_stddev = 0.1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SOH estimation results of proposed model compared with three state-of-the-art methods.

head.ce:title: Enhanced state of health prediction for lithium-ion batteries using a hybrid convolutional–Kolmogorov–Arnold network

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mathematics and Computer Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Hanzhong, Shaanxi Province 723000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Mathematics and Computer Science, Shaanxi University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Mathematics and Computer Science, Shaanxi University of Technology, Shaanxi Province, Hanzhong 723000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reliable estimation of the state of health (SOH) of lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) is essential to ensure the continuous and safe operation of energy-dependent systems. Traditional methods for LIB SOH estimation using neural networks often lacked the flexibility required to handle high-dimensional data, resulting in restricted accuracy. To address this issue, this study proposes a hybrid convolutional neural network (CNN)–Kolmogorov–Arnold network (KAN) model to provide a more accurate estimation of LIB SOH. With its learnable activation functions, KANs overcome the inflexibility challenges of traditional neural networks and have the potential to replace conventional multilayer perceptrons (MLP). In this study, five indirect health features (IHFs) were extracted from the charge–discharge voltage and capacity enhancement curves. To address the issue of weak informative content in IHFs, we try to combine the CNN’s powerful high-dimensional feature extraction capabilities to optimize the IHFs, thereby improving the prediction performance of the KAN. Convolutional layers are employed to extract local features from the IHFs, whereas pooling layers further reduce their dimensionality while preserving crucial information. This optimization enhances the feature set and significantly improves prediction accuracy. Finally, the optimized features are fed into the KAN model for SOH estimation. The SHAP technique was applied to calculate the contribution of each IHF to address the black-box nature and lack of interpretability inherent in data-driven models. This study uses publicly available datasets provided by CALCE, Oxford, as well as the Toyota Research Institute and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) for algorithm validation. The experimental results demonstrate that the CNN–KAN model performs excellently, with an root mean squared error (RMSE) less than 0.007, mean absolute error (MAE) less than 0.005, and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) below 0.006. Compared with the errors in other studies, the results highlight the strong predictive accuracy and reliability of the CNN-KAN model.

ce:section.ce:para: Owing to their extended operational lifespan, absence of memory issues, and outstanding performance in terms of both electrical potential and strength output [1,2], lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are crucial elements in grid-connected power storage and cutting-edge electronic gadgets [3,4]. Their use in electric vehicles (EVs) far surpasses that of other battery types [5]; however, their performance gradually deteriorates [6], potentially leading to safety concerns [7], posing a significant barrier to the widespread adoption of EVs. A precise evaluation of LIBs’ state-of-health (SOH) is vital for the effective functioning of the battery management system (BMS). The enabling of informed decisions helps mitigate the potential for battery failure and guarantees the continuous and secure performance of the entire power storage network [8,9].

Although the SOH is essential for an effective BMS procedure, its precise definition and calculation methods are yet to be clearly established [10]. In most existing studies, two primary approaches have been utilized to assess the SOH: the Ohmic resistance and capacity estimation methods [11]. Among these, capacity estimation is frequently adopted as the representative parameter for SOH [12–13,14] because capacity degradation provides the most direct indication of LIB aging, whereas resistance is highly susceptible to external environmental influences. To measure battery capacity and internal resistance, direct measurement methods can be applied to calculate the SOH. However, these methods are restricted to laboratory conditions and unsuitable for online SOH estimation in practical applications, resulting in relatively limited research on this topic. The SOH is typically assessed using two broad categories of techniques: one that incorporates physical models, such as circuits or electrochemical frameworks, and one that utilizes machine learning or data-driven algorithms to predict battery performance.

Methods Based on Circuit or Electrochemical Models: In this approach, the process of evaluating the health of a battery is treated as a parameter estimation task, wherein the battery model is developed by identifying the most relevant parameters that define its behavior. Fahmy et al. suggested a model combining a dual-adaptive unscented Kalman filter with the Coulomb counting technique to effectively assess both the state of charge (SOC) and SOH of LIBs [15]. Mu et al. first used a battery reference model to compute the SOC [16], and the remaining capacity and internal resistance of the batteries were assessed using differential models. Additionally, a novel strategy was used to explore the SOH of LIBs by analyzing the differences in the voltage behavior observed in various battery cells. To enable the concurrent estimation of the SOC and SOH, an advanced algorithm was proposed by Chen et al. [17] that combined a temperature-variable second-order RC circuit model with a grey wolf optimization (GWO) technique and a dual-adaptive extended Kalman filter for enhanced accuracy. An adaptive PECM was introduced by Huang et al. [18] that incorporated an extended Kalman filter to evaluate the SOC and SOH concurrently. Despite their effectiveness, methods based on these models rely on specific battery characteristics, which restrict their adaptability to dynamic real-time environments. Building a model entails identifying a range of parameters, which results in higher calculation demand, making it difficult to implement these approaches effectively in applications involving time-series data.

Data-Driven Techniques: Unlike methods that require knowledge of battery aging mechanisms, this approach leverages available data to directly construct a model for predicting the SOH without a detailed understanding of an LIB’s internal workings. Consequently, this method has attracted widespread attention [19,20]. Because measuring battery capacity directly is impractical, most recent approaches have focused on using easily observable factors such as voltage, current, and temperature, which provide important perspectives on the general state and functioning of LIBs. The strength of their correlation with the SOH directly influences the accuracy of the estimation model [21–24]. Zhang et al. investigated the difficulties associated with extracting indirect health features (IHFs) from the incremental capacity curve. To overcome these challenges, they proposed an alternative technique that utilizes the voltage-capacity curve to extract these indicators more effectively [25]. He et al. identified the variation coefficient from the local charging voltage and current profiles as an IHF [26]. In a similar approach, Goh and Hui Hwang et al. employed a U-string curvature model that segmented the discharge process into distinct stages based on the curvature of the discharge data, enabling the extraction of additional IHFs [27]. In addition to the selection of IHFs, the choice of model is critical for accurate SOH estimation. Zhang et al. [28] proposed a multi-task learning framework based on convolutional neural network-gated recurrent unit multi-gate mixture, which can manage multiple SOH estimation regression tasks at the same time. LiJun Zhang [29] focused on using the capacity of the battery as a crucial feature for evaluating its SOH. They implemented an LSTM-based approach to estimate the SOH of a battery, offering a tailored model for this purpose. Park et al. [30] developed a predictive approach utilizing a time-series model that adjusts past data inputs in real time to effectively capture evolving trends in battery capacity and health status, addressing their nonlinear progression. Wu et al. [31] utilized the voltage and incremental capacity (IC) curves obtained during the charge–discharge cycle to extract several IHFs and used an LSTM model to estimate the SOH of the battery. Similarly, Waseem et al. [32] merged GWO with Bayesian regularized neural networks to predict the SOH. Zhang et al. [33] proposed a bidirectional long-short-term memory (LSTM-reduction) with a reduction mechanism. The model integrates forward and reverse LSTM structures and has a reduction gate in the LSTM architecture. This gate filters unnecessary data and retains valuable information during processing, providing a new method for accurate prediction of SOH. Data-centric approaches are becoming increasingly popular because they bypass the need to balance computational efficiency with model accuracy. These methods focus on data rather than the detailed electrochemical processes of batteries, allowing them to provide accurate results without requiring specific battery model knowledge. Therefore, this study focused on utilizing a data-driven technique to forecast the SOH of LIBs.

The aforementioned data-driven models exhibit significant limitations despite receiving widespread attention. Single time-series models primarily focus on the inherent trends and periodicity of time-series data and may overlook other critical factors that significantly influence the SOH. Moreover, these models have limited capability to capture the nonlinear information associated with capacity regeneration. In common regression estimation models, accuracy largely depends on the quality of the input features; a well-optimized feature set can substantially improve the model's estimation accuracy. However, the aforementioned studies did not explore feature optimization. In addition, commonly used neural network models are predominantly based on multilayer perceptrons (MLPs), which are limited by their fixed and inflexible activation functions. This lack of flexibility restricts the estimation performance of the model, leading to suboptimal SOH prediction accuracy.

To address the aforementioned issues, this study proposes a hybrid model combining a convolutional neural network (CNN) and a Kolmogorov–Arnold network (KAN) for SOH estimation. A CNN is employed to extract local information from IHFs and reduce dimensionality, thereby enhancing the effective information of features and improving the estimation accuracy. As an emerging neural network architecture, a KAN has the potential to replace MLPs. By using spline functions as activation functions and optimizing these functions through parameterized learning, the KAN overcomes the limitations of MLP networks. This endows the KAN with greater flexibility and improves the estimation accuracy of the model. Moreover, some researchers have applied the KAN to time-series analyses, demonstrating its ability to capture complex temporal dependencies [34]. Thus, the novelty of this study lies in combining the strengths of the CNN and KAN to create an SOH prediction model. The KAN’s flexible nonlinear fitting ability enables it to accurately capture the underlying relationships in the data, and the CNN’s powerful spatial feature extraction capability enhances the KAN's prediction performance. This method improves the accuracy and reliability of the SOH prediction for LIBs and can assist the BMS in implementing effective measures to help the safe and efficient operation of devices. Furthermore, to address the black-box effect and lack of transparency in data-driven models, the SHAP method was employed to evaluate the contribution of each IHF and analyze the dependencies among IHFs, offering a reference for future research on IHF interactions. The main contributions of this study are summarized as follows:

(1) The application of the KAN model focuses on estimating the SOH of LIBs, aiming to improve the estimation reliability and overall performance evaluation. The MLP model lays the foundation for neural networks and remains an essential component of artificial intelligence. The KAN model addresses the shortcomings of the MLP, such as its poor interpretability and inflexibility, and offers potential advantages as an alternative. The potential of the KAN method is examined for the evaluation of the SOH of LIBs, demonstrating its ability to provide more reliable and precise estimations of battery conditions than existing approaches.
(2) A CNN is used to further optimize the IHFs, enhance the useful information of the features, and improve the KAN model.

(3) To address the lack of transparency in data-driven models, the SHAP model is employed to interpret each IHF and analyze the importance of each feature. In addition, this paper explores the dependencies between different features and analyzes the interactions between them in the model, providing a reference for future research on feature selection.

This remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 covers the preprocessing of the dataset, which involves calculating the SOH of LIBs and identifying key IHFs. Section 3 introduces the theoretical foundation and techniques used in this study. In Section 4, we present the experimental results, provide a comprehensive analysis, and verify the proposed method via a model comparison. The conclusions are presented in Section 5. The overall structure of the study is shown in Fig. 1.

Considering the straightforwardness of gathering capacity-related data and its strong correlation with a battery's overall condition, a method focusing on capacity was applied in this study to calculate the SOH of LIBs [35]. The formula is
(1)
SOH =
Q c
Q rate
× 100 %
where
Q c
is the current maximum capacity and Qrate is the nominal capacity.

This study uses publicly available lithium-ion battery datasets provided by CALCE (CS2_35, CS2_36, CS2_37), Oxford (Cell1, Cell2, Cell3), as well as the MIT dataset for algorithm validation. In the MIT dataset, we used the 2018–04–12 batch data (channel_id:1, 20, 17), The experimental process of the battery is described in Ref. [36–38]. Fig. 2 shows the SOH degradation curve, and
Figs. 3–5 show the charge voltage, capacity increment, and charge temperature curves, respectively, for the CS2_35 battery.

As a predictive feature for LIBs, the IHF is crucial for achieving accurate SOH estimation. Existing research shows that chemical reactions occurring in LIBs during use lead to a continuous decrease in their capacity and a gradual decline in their SOH [39]. Current research methods have mostly extracted IHFs from the voltage and IC curves during the battery charging and discharging processes [40,41]. Figs. 2 and 4 show the charging and discharging voltage contours of CS2_35, respectively, where CC and CV represent the constant-current and constant-voltage charging phases, respectively. The differences between these phases is evident. In this study, IHF1, IHF2, IHF3, and IHF4 were extracted from the charging and discharging voltage curves to represent aging features.

IHF1 quantifies the time required for the voltage of an LIB to increase from a lower value (U1) to a higher value (U2) during the charging process. For the CALCE and Oxford dataset, the starting voltage (U1) is set at 3.8 V, and the ending voltage (U2) is set at 4.1 V. For the MIT dataset, the starting voltage (U1) is set at 3 V, and the ending voltage (U2) is set at 3.5 V. As depicted in Fig. 2, with an increase in the number of battery aging cycles, the battery charging curve became more pronounced, resulting in a gradual decline in the IHF1 value. The formula for this metric is
(2)
IHF 1 =
t
U 2
−
t
U 1
where
t
U 2
is to the time it takes for the voltage to rise to U2, and
t
U 1
is the time when the voltage reaches U1 during the charging cycle.

IHF2 quantifies the time interval required for the voltage of the LIB to decline from a value of U3 to U4 during the discharge procedure. For the CALCE and Oxford dataset,U3 is defined as 4.0 V and U4 as 3.6 V. For the MIT dataset,U3 is defined as 3.5 V and U4 as 3 V. As shown in Fig. 4, with an increase in aging cycles, IHF2 gradually decreased and is expressed as
(3)
IHF 2 =
t
U 3
−
t
U 4
where
t
U 3
represents the time corresponding to a voltage of U3 and
t
U 4
represents the time corresponding to a voltage of U4.

For IHF1 and IHF2, we randomly selected multiple sets of IHF thresholds within the voltage range of the charge and discharge start and stop voltages and extracted the IHF corresponding to each battery at different thresholds. Then, the Pearson coefficient between each IHF threshold set and the SOH was calculated for each battery, along with the average Pearson coefficient of all batteries in each dataset at different IHF thresholds. Finally, the threshold with the highest average Pearson coefficient in each dataset was selected as the IHF cutoff value to determine the optimal threshold. U1, U2, U3, and U4 are the thresholds obtained for IHF1 and IHF2.

IHF3 is defined as the time required for the battery to charge at a steady current. Over time, as the battery underwent more cycles and deterioration, the charging duration decreased. Therefore, this time interval during constant-current charging was considered as IHF3. The following equation illustrates this concept:
(4)
IHF 3 =
t
U = 4.2 v
where
t
U = 4.2 v
denotes the time required for the battery to attain a voltage of 4.2 V, with the previous phase representing the initial charging phase under constant-current conditions.

IHF4 is the length of time for a battery to charge to a fixed voltage. As the battery underwent more aging cycles, the time required for this phase increased and was inversely correlated with the overall SOH of the battery. The mathematical representation of this metric is
(5)
IHF 4 =
t
charge_end
−
t
U = 4.2 v
where
t
charge_end
is the moment at which the charging phase ends and
t
U = 4.2 v
is the moment at which the constant-voltage charging phase begins.

IHF5 is identified as the highest point on the curve representing the IC as shown in Fig. 3, where the behavior of the curve is plotted for the battery. Notably, with the progression of the aging cycles, this peak value progressively declined. This peak on the curve can be mathematically expressed as
(6)
IHF 5 = max ( dV / dQ )
where dV/dQ represents the IC value. To assess the relevance of the IHFs, the Pearson correlation coefficient was employed as the method of evaluation. The equation used for this calculation is
(7)
ρ

represent the IHF and SOH values, respectively, and

y
̅
represent the mean IHF and SOH values, respectively, and
ρ
i
denotes the Pearson correlation coefficient between the i-th feature and SOH. (
Tables 1–2) presents the Pearson correlation coefficients for the IHF and SOH. The closer the absolute value of the Pearson's correlation is to 1, the stronger the correlation. As shown in the table, the absolute values of the correlation coefficients between each IHF and the SOH were greater than 0.8, indicating the effectiveness of the selected IHFs. In this study, the five IHFs were extracted as features and simultaneously input into the CNN-KAN model to estimate the SOH of LIBs.

To investigate how machine learning models predict the SOH of LIBs, the SHAP framework was applied. This technique uses Shapley values originating from cooperative game theory to clarify the behavior of a model. These values assess the average contribution of each individual feature to the overall prediction. SHAP is characterized by the use of an additive method to attribute feature importance that complies with specific properties designed to improve model interpretability [42]. This approach assigns an importance score to each feature to reflect its influence on model predictions. The explanatory model for this method is
(8)
g
z
′
=
φ
0
+
∑
i = 1
M
φ
i
z
i
′
where z′∈{0,1}M is a joint vector indicating the presence of the i-th feature. In this framework, the explanation model uses the simplified input x', which is mapped to the original input x through the mapping function
h
x
x
′
= x
, ensuring that
g (
z
′
) ≈ f (
h
x
(
z
′
) )
, where
z
′
≈
x
′
. An explanation model is constructed and trained to interpret the original model f. Since SHAP measures the importance of features by comparing the prediction results of the model with and without the feature, the SHAP value
φ
i
(i.e., the feature importance value) is computed as the weighted average of all possible differences as
(9)
φ
i
=
∑
S ⊆ N {
x
i
}

represents the set of features excluding xi, S is the subset of features excluding the i-th feature, and
f

and

( S )
represent the model's prediction results when the i-th feature is included and excluded, respectively. It is important to note that SHAP will only be considered an additive feature attribution method if
φ
0
=
f
x
( φ )
. According to the SHAP properties, the prediction value of the original model for each data sample is equal to the sum of the SHAP values of all the features for that sample. Consequently, the SHAP value quantifies the impact of each feature on the predicted output of the model, reflecting its specific role in the decision-making process.

The role of the IHF as an input component for estimating the SOH of LIBs is critical. To ensure precise predictions, selecting the correct input features is a key component for machine learning models. For feature extraction, this study employed convolutional layers within a CNN to capture spatial patterns in the data, the principle of which was derived from [43]. The corresponding equation for this approach is
(10)
x
i
l
= f (
W
i
l
*
X
l − 1
+
b
i
l
)
where
x
i
l
represents the i-th feature vector at layer l, and f is the function used to activate the values. The weight associated with the i-th filter in layer l is denoted by
W
i
l
, and the symbol * represents the convolution operation. The output of the previous layer, labeled
X
l − 1
, is utilized to compute the result, and
b
i
l
corresponds to the bias applied when transitioning from layer l-1 to layer l. Following the convolution operation, the pooling layer is typically introduced to decrease the spatial size of the feature maps while maintaining key information. The mathematical expression for this process is
(11)
y
i
l + 1
j
= max
x
i
l
k
where
y
i
l + 1
( j )
signifies the value located in the i-th feature matrix at the (l+1)th layer after the pooling process is applied. The term
x
i
l
( k )
represents the corresponding value from the i-th matrix at layer l, and j denotes the specific pooling region being considered.

The KAN, introduced in 2024 by the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, is a novel network architecture based on the principles outlined in [44]. KAN represents a novel development in the field of neural networks and is primarily distinguished by its versatility and transparency [45]. Unlike traditional models, such as MLP, KAN does not rely on fixed activation functions. Instead, it employs learnable one-variable functions that enhance its ability to adapt to complex data patterns. This characteristic gives the KAN an advantage over MLPs, improving both its predictive accuracy and computational efficiency while maintaining a high level of model explainability.

Kolmogorov’s theorem posits that it is possible to represent any continuous function involving several variables, such as the aggregation of a small number of independent continuous functions, each involving only one regressor. For an uninterrupted mapping with n variables (x₁, x₂, …, xn), this theorem ensures the existence of specific continuous functions gi and hij that make this representation possible in the form
(12)
f
x 1 , x 2 , … , xn
=
∑
i = 1
2 n + 1
g
i
∑
j = 1
n
h
ij
x
j
where n is the number of input variables or the dimensionality. The external function
g
i
represents a continuous function of a sole parameter and operates based on the combined effects produced by the internal processes. Each internal function
h
ij
x
j
, also continuous with respect to a single variable, handles a particular input variable xj and plays a role in the overall sum for each external function. The values of i are within the range of 1–2 n + 1, and j spans from 1 to n.

According to this theory, intricate multivariable functions can be represented as the sum of simpler functions with a single variable, providing a new approach to neural network design. The KAN model shown in Fig. 6 was taken from reference [46]. The system is organized into several distinct levels, where the elements are represented by nodes and connected. Nodes in the network process input from the previous layer and apply nonlinear transformations using adjustable single-variable functions on the connecting edges. Within the KAN, the weights are modeled as functions of a single variable, and their forms are modified during the training phase. This architecture ensures that each layer's output is influenced not only by incoming signals but also by parameters that are optimized to match various data patterns, thereby strengthening the model's capability to recognize detailed trends present in the dataset.

The KAN model typically has a high complexity and contains a large number of parameters and neurons. Therefore, sparsifying the KAN model is very important with the aim of limiting its complexity and making it more concise. In this study, the activation function of the KAN model was set to a rectified linear unit (ReLU), which has the advantage of sparsity. This function is defined as
ReLu = max
0 , x
. This implies that when the input value is less than zero, the output is zero, and when the input value is greater than zero, the output is equal to the input. This property causes some neurons in the network to output zero, leading to sparse activation. Because the ReLU function outputs zero when the input is negative, it reduces the computational load of the neural network, as there is no need to compute for the inactive neurons. This also helps reduce the complexity of the neural network, potentially improving the training speed. Setting the activation function to the ReLU can help sparsify the activation of the neural network. However, if the goal is to make the network weights sparse, regularization is typically required. For this, we referred to the literature [47]. For the KAN model, the following regularization terms were used.

The cross-entropy loss is defined as
(13)
L
ce
= −
∑
i = 1
N
∑
c = 1
C
y

log (
p
i , c
)

The KAN loss with regularization is defined as
(14)
L
reg
=
L
ce
+ λ (
μ

∑
l = 1
L − 1
Φ

with
(15)
S

|

1

|
Φ |
1
)

The ReLU activation function can help sparsify the activation of the neural network, whereas regularization can sparsify the network's weights. By combining both, the KAN model can be simplified, thereby reducing its complexity and improving its computational efficiency. Additionally, this model has defined an early stopping callback, which halts the training if the val_loss does not improve for 10 consecutive epochs, and restores the model's best weights when training stops. This can significantly reduce the running time.

The proposed CNN–KAN consists of four parts: input, CNN, KAN, and output. The input layer receives the LIB IHF data and processes them into a format suitable for the CNN input. The CNN is composed of convolutional and pooling layers, including two 1D convolutional layers that use the ReLU activation function, introducing nonlinearity into the network and enabling it to learn more complex features. L2 regularization is applied to prevent overfitting by constraining the size of the convolutional kernel weights. The convolutional layers are responsible for extracting local features from the input data. The pooling layer performs global average pooling on each feature map to reduce the dimensionality of the output of the convolutional layer by averaging each channel, thereby reducing the complexity of the model. This operation effectively reduces the number of parameters and retains important global information. The CNN is used to process the time-series information in the LIB data and extract the spatial features, which are then passed to the KAN layer. The KAN consists of two layers; the first layer is responsible for learning the complex relationships between features, and the second layer generates the final prediction results. The parameters for model training are listed in Table 3. The model proposed in this study combines the advantages of the CNN network in extracting spatial features with the ability of the KAN to handle complex and nonlinear data, achieving LIB SOH modeling, which is effective for handling time-series data and complex feature interactions. The framework of the proposed model is illustrated in Fig. 7.

In addition, this paper discusses the complexity of the CNN-KAN model. Both the CNN and KAN in this model consist of two layers. According to reference [48], the time complexity of each convolutional layer is
O (
M
2
∙
K
2
∙
C
in
∙
C
out
)
, and according to reference [49], the time complexity of each KAN layer is
O (
n
l
n
l + 1
)
. Therefore, the overall time complexity of this paper is
O
M
2
∙
K
2
∙
C
in
∙
C
out
+ O (
n
l
n
l + 1
)
, where M is the side length of the feature map output by each convolutional kernel, K is the side length of each convolutional kernel,
C
in
is the number of channels of each convolutional kernel,
C
out
is the number of output channels,
n
l
is the number of neurons in the l-th layer, and
n
l + 1
is the number of neurons in the l+ 1-th layer.

To evaluate the performance of the proposed approach in forecasting the SOH of lithium-ion cells, the three datasets was split, and the results are presented in Table 2. The partitioned dataset was then input into the proposed CNN–KAN model for validation.

Because of the varying numerical ranges of the IHF values of LIBs that hinder the model's ability to capture the underlying data patterns, normalization was performed to eliminate discrepancies arising from different data dimensions and improve the model's predictive performance. In this study, the extracted IHFs and LIB SOH were normalized using min–max normalization, ensuring that the data values were scaled within the range [0,1]. The mathematical expression is,
(16)
x
*
=
x −
x
min
x
max
−
x
min
wherex
max and x
min represent the maximum and minimum values of the sample data, respectively.

Three evaluation metrics were selected to assess the accuracy of the model: mean absolute error (MAE), mean absolute percentage error (MAPE), and root mean square error (RMSE). The mathematical expressions for these metrics are
(17)
MAE =
1
N
∙
∑
i = 1
N
|
y
i
ˆ
−
y
i
|
(18)
RMSE =
1
N
∑
i = 1
N
(
y
i
ˆ
−
y
i
)
2
(19)
MAPE =
∑
i = 1
N
y
i
ˆ
−
y
i
y
i
N
∙ 100 %
where
y
i
ˆ
represents the predicted SOH value,
y
i
denotes the true SOH value, and N is the number of samples in the test set.
Based on dataset partitioning, the extracted proposed IHFs were used as inputs to the CNN–KAN model to predict the SOH of LIBs. The model parameters are listed in Table 3.

The MLP model has long been a fundamental architecture in the realm of neural networks, but the KAN has gained attention as a potential substitute. For the purpose of this study, the KAN framework was applied instead of the MLP during the training process. A side-by-side evaluation of both models was performed, and the findings are listed in Table 4. In the case of Set 1, the KAN model demonstrated a significant improvement, reducing the RMSE by 41.28 %, MAE by 39.1 %, and MAPE by 32.2 % compared with the MLP model. Similar improvements in the error metrics were also observed for all datasets in this paper, further supporting the effectiveness of the KAN approach over that of the conventional MLP architecture.

The performance of the proposed CNN–KAN model was compared with that of other architectures, namely, CNN, KAN, and GRU, during the training phase. When tested on Set 1, the CNN–KAN model resulted in significant improvements, with reductions of 64.3 % in RMSE, 59.2 % in MAE, and 60.55 % in MAPE compared with those of the KAN model. In Set 2, the CNN–KAN model demonstrated a decrease of 63.01 % in RMSE, 53.8 % in MAE, and 57.5 % in MAPE. The CNN–KAN model also outperformed the KAN model for all datasets in this paper. These results, which show considerable reductions in the prediction errors for all datasets, indicate that the integration of CNN's spatial feature extraction capability plays a crucial role in boosting the overall accuracy of the KAN model predictions.

To further evaluate the effectiveness of the CNN–KAN model, a direct comparison was performed with the traditional CNN model. The results presented in Table 4 demonstrate that the CNN–KAN model provided a significant improvement for Set 1, with reductions of 19.4 % in RMSE, 16.11 % in MAE, and 21.1 % in MAPE relative to that of the CNN model. Similarly, for other datasets, the CNN–KAN model displayed substantial enhancements in prediction quality, emphasizing its superior performance when dealing with intricate and high-dimensional datasets, resulting in more accurate predictions.

From Table 4, in terms of training time, the CNN-KAN model exhibits a longer training duration, which is likely due to the increased time complexity introduced by the KAN module. Among the other individual models, the KAN model demonstrates a longer training time than the CNN-KAN model, as the latter utilizes an early stopping callback function, which effectively reduces the training time. But in the Toyota dataset, due to the large size of the samples, the model still exhibited a longer training time. Additionally, the training time for GRU and LSTM is greater than that of the CNN-KAN model, and their prediction errors are also larger. The model with the shortest training time is BLS, but its prediction accuracy is not as good as that of the CNN-KAN model. The error metrics shows that the predictions from the individual models are prone to fluctuations and lack stability, largely owing to the variability of the battery cells. In comparison, the CNN–KAN model exhibited far more reliable prediction performance, with the highest RMSE of 0.006084 and MAE of 0.004854, suggesting that its predictions remained consistent and exhibited favorable error distribution and convergence behavior. Figs. 8 and 9 presents a visual comparison of the predictive accuracy of each model by displaying the prediction curves, error plots, and fitting graphs across different datasets. It is evident that the SOH estimates of the CNN–KAN model were the closest to the true values, whereas the predictions from the other models showed marked discrepancies. Moreover, the error chart demonstrates that the CNN–KAN model achieved the smallest absolute errors, and its fitting curve R2 values surpassed 0.98, emphasizing the superiority of the method for evaluating the SOH of LIBs.

To investigate the accuracy of the SOH prediction results in this study, the experimental results were compared with those of other studies. Table 5 presents a comparison between the proposed model and three advanced SOH estimation methods for the CALCE dataset. The results indicate that the CS2–35 battery in Ref. [50] exhibited the lowest errors, whereas the proposed model provided the most accurate results for the CS2–36 and CS2–37 batteries. Additionally, the average RMSE and MAE of the proposed model were 0.005776 and 0.004376, respectively, both of which were the smallest. This demonstrated that the proposed model provided relatively accurate SOH prediction results for LIBs.

To further investigate the impact of health features on the battery SOH assessment results, the SHAP method was employed to analyze the CNN–KAN model. The Shapley values of various features were obtained, and the average Shapley value of each health feature was determined by calculating the average of the absolute Shapley values across all feature points within each category. This average value was used to characterize the influence of IHFs on the SOH assessment results. Fig. 10 presents the SHAP values and feature value results for the IHF of each battery. From the feature values in the figure, it can be observed that for all dataset, two key features— IHF2, IHF3—are identified as influencing the battery SOH. Among these, IHF3 has the highest influence, followed by IHF2 in terms of feature importance, which indicated their significant role in the CNN-KAN model. Based on this, the Shapley value scatter plots were generated for the internal feature points of each IHF. The scatter points in the plot represent the distribution of the Shapley values for each IHF, with the magnitude of the Shapley value indicating the contribution of each IHF to the battery SOH. As shown in Fig. 9, the primary features, IHF2 and IHF3, had a positive impact on the battery SOH. As the feature values increased, the Shapley values also increased, resulting in a higher corresponding SOH. Fig. 11 illustrates the feature-dependent relationship between IHF2 and IHF4, where the IHF2 value exceeded 608 and the Shapley value became positive, exerting a positive influence on the SOH evaluation results. As the value of IHF2 increased, the value of IHF4 decreased, indicating a significant negative correlation. Fig. 12 illustrates the feature-dependent relationship between IHF1 and IHF3, where the value of IHF3 exceeded 5375 and the Shapley value became positive, exerting a positive influence on the SOH evaluation results. Additionally, as the value of IHF1 increased, the value of IHF3 increased, indicating a positive correlation between the two.

The above analysis demonstrates that the SHAP method provides insight into the influence of IHFs on the battery SOH, enhancing the interpretability of the evaluation method. This approach offers a theoretical foundation for practical applications in BMS.

To overcome the challenges in predicting the SOH of LIBs with high accuracy, this study proposed a sophisticated approach based on the CNN–KAN framework. The proposed methodology identifies and extracts five key indirect health features (IHF1–IHF5) derived from the voltage and IC change profiles of LIBs during different cycles. These IHFs were fed into the CNN–KAN structure, allowing the generation of more precise SOH estimates. Validation experiments conducted using the three datasets (CALCE, Oxford and MIT) revealed that the CNN–KAN approach significantly outperformed other conventional techniques, including CNN, KAN, MLP, and GRU, in terms of both prediction precision and stability. Across all data subsets in this paper, the CNN–KAN model consistently delivered outstanding performance. This highlights the effectiveness of the KAN integration, where the CNN’s capacity for spatial feature extraction is effectively combined with the KAN’s ability to handle intricate relationships in data, leading to substantially better SOH prediction accuracy. To further enhance model transparency, SHAP analysis was employed to examine how each IHF contributed to the SOH predictions, revealing the underlying feature interactions and improving the interpretability of the overall model.

Yongzheng Feng: Writing – review & editing. Yangke Cui: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
